230	
231	- Prefer anchors the **directive names** (the plan agent/reviewer identified them as Mathlib results).
232	- If you are not confident the exact statement is in Mathlib, do **not** mark `\mathlibok` — write the block without it (or leave the `\uses{}` for the reviewer to resolve) and flag it under "Notes for Plan Agent" so the reviewer can verify against Lean.
233	- Never put `\mathlibok` on one of the project's **own** to-be-proved declarations. That marker is for Mathlib-supplied results only; `\leanok` (project proofs) is never yours to add.
234	
235	## Reading references
236	
237	You write mathematics, not literature criticism — your prose must be grounded in authoritative sources, not your training memory. Before composing any new declaration block:
238	
239	1. **Read `references/summary.md`** to see the index of sources the project already has. Sources directly relevant to your chapter are your first stop.
240	2. **Read every reference file your directive names** under its `## References` section. The plan agent named those because the chapter needs them.
241	3. **Read anything in `references/summary.md` your directive didn't name but that is clearly relevant** to your chapter (same area, same theorem, same construction).
242	
243	Do NOT write content from your training memory when you could ground it in a reference instead. When the references say one thing and your memory says another, trust the references.
244	
245	## Dispatching a reference-retriever (when the project's sources don't have what you need)
246	
247	If, while drafting, you discover that the chapter needs material the project's `references/` doesn't contain, **dispatch a `reference-retriever` mid-session** rather than guessing or papering over the gap.
248	
249	Conditions for dispatch:
250	
251	- The directive named a source (`Smith 2018`, an arXiv ID) that isn't in `references/` yet.
252	- The required content is in a known textbook (Hartshorne, Vakil, Stacks Project, etc.) but the project hasn't summarized the relevant chapter.
253	- You need a Mathlib-adjacent source (nLab, math overflow, Stacks Project) the directive didn't anticipate.
254	
255	Dispatch (Bash; treat as blocking and await the report; in your write-domain only if it includes `references/**`):
256	
257	```
258	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
259	  --name reference-retriever \
260	  --slug <kebab-slug-for-the-source> \
261	  --directive-file .archon/logs/iter-NNN/<your-slug>/reference-retriever-<child-slug>-directive.md \
262	  --write-domain 'references/**'
263	```
264	
265	Write the directive file first; the directive format is documented in `.archon/subagents/reference-retriever.md`. The retriever returns when the new summary is on disk; **then resume writing**, citing the new reference.
266	
267	If your invocation's recorded write-domain does NOT include `references/**` (your `parent.write_domain` in `dispatch.jsonl`), the dispatch will be rejected. In that case, STOP writing the affected section, report the missing source in "Notes for Plan Agent", and finish the parts of the chapter you can still write. The plan agent will re-dispatch you next iter with the broader write-domain.
268	
269	## Workflow
270	
271	1. Read your directive completely.
272	2. **Read `references/summary.md`** and every reference your directive points at; also read sibling-chapter material that informs cross-references. Track which local files you actually opened — you will need their paths for the `% SOURCE:` parenthetical.
273	3. Read the target chapter currently on disk to see what's already there.
274	4. Plan the edits: which blocks to add, which to revise. Decide where each new block goes in the chapter's existing flow. For each block that derives from an external source, identify the specific local `references/<file>.md` containing the verbatim statement (and proof, if applicable). If no local file covers the statement, list it as "retrieval needed" for the next step.
275	5. **For every "retrieval needed" item, dispatch a `reference-retriever`** (see above) and **wait** for it to return. THEN open and read the newly-written `references/<slug>.md` before drafting the citing block. Do not draft citation blocks against pending or imagined sources.
276	6. Make the edits. For each citation block: copy `% SOURCE QUOTE:` and `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` content character-by-character from the local reference file — original language, original notation, every word. Do not transcribe from a window of the file you "remember reading"; have the file open and copy.
277	7. Verify the file is still valid LaTeX at a glance (no unmatched begin/end, balanced braces in `\label`/`\uses`/`\lean`). Spot-check that every `% SOURCE:` line has a non-empty `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical and that the named file exists.
278	8. **Verify dependencies with `leandag`** (see the section below): confirm your chapter introduced no broken `\uses{}` and left none of your blocks unintentionally isolated. Fix the edges and re-check.
279	9. Write your report. List under "References consulted" every local file you opened in step 2 and step 5.
280	
281	## Verifying dependencies with leandag
282	
283	`leandag` parses the real Lean ↔ blueprint dependency DAG, so use it to check your `\uses{}` instead of trusting your memory of the graph. It is read-only on the blueprint (it never edits your chapter); run it after you make edits:
284	
285	```
286	leandag build --json                       # report: unknown_uses, unmatched_lean, isolated count
287	leandag query --isolated --chapter <c>     # isolated nodes in your chapter (no edges in or out)
288	leandag show isolated                       # all isolated nodes, project-wide
289	```
290	
291	`leandag` is on the same PATH as `archon`; it is the one tool you use to query the DAG.
292	
293	What to do with the output, scoped to **your chapter**:
294	
295	- **`unknown_uses` naming a label in your chapter** → you wrote a `\uses{}` pointing at a label that doesn't exist. Fix the label, or — if the target genuinely belongs in your chapter and the directive calls for it — add the missing declaration. If the missing label lives in another chapter, leave a correct `\uses{}` to it and note it under "Notes for Plan Agent" (you don't edit that chapter).
296	- **A block you wrote shows up as isolated** → it has no `\uses{}` out and nothing uses it. This is almost always a *missing edge*: add the `\uses{}` that ties it to the declarations its statement/proof actually depends on (and check whether a sibling result should `\uses{}` it). Only treat an isolated block as removable when your directive explicitly authorized removing it.
297	- **Removal** (directive-authorized only): delete the orphaned block cleanly and record it in your report. Never remove the goal or a block whose `\lean{}` still names a live Lean declaration.
298	
299	Keep `leandag` edits inside your assigned chapter — fixing a dependency that lives in another chapter is a "Notes for Plan Agent" item, not a cross-chapter edit.
300	
301	## Logging
302	
303	Write your report to `.archon/task_results/blueprint-writer-<slug>.md` (or the parent-aware path under `task_results/<parent-slug>/` when invoked nested — your invocation prompt names the exact path).
304	
305	```markdown

308	## Slug
309	<slug>
310

317

320	- **Added theorem** `\theorem`/`\label{thm:foo}` — <one line on statement>
321	  - Proof sketch added: <Y/N + brief shape>
322	- **Revised** `<existing label>` — <one line on what changed>
323	- **Fixed dependencies** `<label>` — corrected/added `\uses{...}` (was isolated / had a broken ref per leandag).
324	- **Removed** `<label>` — orphaned isolated block, removal authorized by directive.
325	- ...
326	
327	## Cross-references introduced
328	- `\uses{thm:bar}` added in proof of `\thm:foo` — verify `thm:bar` exists in <chapter or this same one>
329	- ...
330	
331	## References consulted
332	<every local file under `references/` you OPENED and READ this session, with a one-line note on what you took from each. The plan agent and the reviewer use this list to verify that every `% SOURCE:` parenthetical points at a file you actually read. Omit this section only when you wrote zero citation blocks (Archon-original chapter).>
333	- `references/hartshorne-III-5.md` — verbatim quote for `\thm:smooth_criterion` (statement + proof).
334	- `references/stacks-tag-01V4.md` — verbatim quote for `\def:etale_morphism`.
335

338

341	- ...

1	# Archon Project
2	
3	You are one of: the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (per descriptor in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.
4	
5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	When instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**:
8	
9	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` override Archon's global prompts.
10	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.
11	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project.
12	
13	## Skills
14	
15	- **archon-lean4** — installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source). Provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, etc.
16	
17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.
20	
21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. Supports DeepSeek, Kimi (OpenAI-compatible via `--provider kimi`), Kimi (Anthropic-compatible via `--provider kimi-anthropic`), OpenRouter, OpenAI, and Gemini; defaults to `--provider auto` which picks the best available key automatically. **Requires at least one key in env** (`DEEPSEEK_API_KEY` / `MOONSHOT_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` / `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY`) — check via `env | grep -E "DEEPSEEK|MOONSHOT|OPENROUTER|OPENAI|GEMINI"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. Both `kimi` and `kimi-anthropic` use the same `MOONSHOT_API_KEY`; the latter speaks Moonshot's Anthropic-compatible Messages endpoint and supports `--think`. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).
25	
26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)
47	
48	## Key Files & Permissions
49	
50	All Archon state files are in `.archon/`. The table below covers **phase agents** (plan, prover, review) and the **user**. Subagent permissions are NOT a fixed row — they are descriptor-driven (`write_domain` glob in the subagent's frontmatter, enforced at dispatch time). See "Subagent permissions" after the table.
51	
52	| File | Plan | Prover | Review | User |
53	|------|------|--------|--------|------|
54	| `PROGRESS.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
55	| `STRATEGY.md` | read + write | — | — | read |
56	| `USER_HINTS.md` | **loop-managed** (captured + cleared by the CLI; injected into the plan prompt) | — | — | write |
57	| `task_pending.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
58	| `task_done.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
59	| `task_results/<file>.md` | read (collect) | write (own file) | read only | read |
60	| `proof-journal/` | read | — | **write** | read |
61	| `PROJECT_STATUS.md` | read | — | **write** (Knowledge Base only — session log lives in `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`) | read |
62	| `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` | **write** (this iter only) | — | read (recent window injected) | read |
63	| `iter/iter-NNN/review.md` | read (recent window injected) | — | **write** (this iter only) | read |
64	| `iter/iter-NNN/objectives.md` | optional write | — | read | read |
65	| `archon-protected.yaml` | read | read | read | **write** |
66	| `.lean` files | — | write (own file; frozen protected signatures) | — | write (via comments) |
67	| `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` | **write** (informal prose, `\lean{...}` hints) | read | **write** (semantic markers: `\mathlibok`, `% NOTE:`, `\lean{...}` corrections — NOT `\leanok`) | read |
68	| `analogies/<slug>.md` | read (when relevant) | — | — | read |
69	| `TO_USER.md` | **write** (shared notice board) | **write** (shared notice board) | **write** (shared notice board) | read |
70	
71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
77	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.
78	
79	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).
80	
81	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise.
82	
83	## Protected Declarations
84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
86	
87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
88	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.
90	
91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	Archon talks back to the user through **`TO_USER.md`** — a single, *persistent* notice board surfaced as a UI banner. It is **shared and writable by every agent** (plan, prover, review). It is no longer cleared at the start of each review phase; content survives across iters until an agent prunes it. The discipline every writer follows:
112	
113	- **Concise**: the whole file stays ≤ 2–3 short bullets. It is a banner, not a log.
114	- **Relevant now**: before adding anything, read the current content and **delete any bullet that is no longer true at this point** (the blocker was resolved, the decision was overtaken, the credential got set). Never let it accumulate stale notices.
115	- **Notice board, never a question queue**: the loop is autonomous and may run unattended for many iters. Never write "awaiting your decision", an options menu, a "where to reply", or "default to X if no reply" — anything implying the loop is paused for a human. State decisions as *made* and blockers as *worked-around-as-far-as-possible*; the user steers asynchronously via `USER_HINTS.md`.
116	
117	## Agent Roles
118	
119	### Plan Agent
120	
121	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
122	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
123	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.
124	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.
125	- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.
126	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
127	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.
128	
129	### Prover Agent
130	
131	- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
132	- Your **active mode** is injected directly into your invocation prompt — read it carefully, it defines your goal, workflow, and constraints for this session.
133	- When your mode specifies `read_blueprint: true`, you are permitted to **read** your assigned blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<your_slug>.tex`) for the informal proof sketch. You may never write to blueprint chapters.
134	- Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
135	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — never edit another agent's file.
136	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.
137	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.
138	
139	### Subagents
140	
141	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).
142	
143	### Review Agent
144	
145	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.
146	- Read `proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data.
147	- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).
148	- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only — session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).
149	- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\notready` removal. Do NOT touch `\leanok`.
150	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.
151	
152	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
153	
154	These run automatically between phases:
155	
156	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.
157	- **`sync_leanok`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.
158	- **`blueprint-doctor`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\ref` / `\uses` / `\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.

24	\section{Setup and motivation}
25	\label{sec:quot_setup}
26	
27	Throughout this chapter, let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme, \(\pi : X \to S\) a
28	projective morphism, \(L\) a relatively very ample line bundle on \(X\) over \(S\),
29	and \(E\) a coherent sheaf on \(X\). The Quot construction packages the
30	\(T\)-flat coherent quotients \(E_T \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\) of the
31	pull-back \(E_T\) to \(X_T = X \times_S T\), modulo the equivalence
32	\(\langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle = \langle \mathcal{F}', q'\rangle\) defined by
33	\(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\). Fixing the Hilbert polynomial
34	\(\Phi(\lambda) = \chi(\mathcal{F}|_{X_t}(\lambda)) \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\)
35	(constant on connected \(T\) by flatness)
36	stratifies the Quot functor into pieces \(\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\), each of
37	which Grothendieck proved is representable by a projective \(S\)-scheme.
38	This chapter develops the foundations of that picture: the Hilbert
39	polynomial (\cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}), the Quot functor
40	(\cref{def:quot_functor}), and the Grassmannian scheme
41	(\cref{def:grassmannian_scheme}) with its representability
42	(\cref{thm:grassmannian_representable}).

180	      \(\mathcal{O}_{X_T}\)-linear homomorphism, where \(E_T\) is the pull-back
181	      of \(E\) along \(X_T \to X\);

194	\section{The Grassmannian scheme}
195	\label{sec:grassmannian_scheme}
196	
197	The Quot construction proceeds by embedding \(\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\) as a
198	locally closed subfunctor of a Grassmannian over \(S\). Since Mathlib carries
199	no Grassmannian \emph{scheme} (only a finite-rank-quotient variety in
200	linear algebra), we package the construction here.

239	  Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme and let \(V\) be a locally free
240	  \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-module of rank \(r\). For an integer \(1 \le d \le r\), the
241	  \emph{Grassmannian of rank-\(d\) quotients of \(V\)} is the functor
242	  \[
243	    \mathrm{Grass}(V, d) : (\mathrm{Sch}/S)^{op} \longrightarrow \mathbf{Set},
244	    \qquad
245	    T \longmapsto \{ \langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle :
246	    q : V_T \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F},\; \mathcal{F}
247	    \text{ locally free of rank } d \}
248	  \]
249	  with equivalence \(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\). Concretely \(\mathrm{Grass}(V, d) =
250	  \Quot^{d,\mathcal{O}_S}_{V/S/S}\) (the Quot functor for \(X = S\), \(E = V\),
251	  constant Hilbert polynomial \(\Phi = d\)).
252	
253	  Structurally \(\mathrm{Grass}(V, d)\) is \emph{defined} as this Quot functor
254	  (\cref{def:quot_functor}) of the special case \(X = S\), \(E = V\),
255	  \(\Phi = d\) -- not as a second, independently-built functor. Fixing the
256	  definition this way makes the identity
257	  \(\mathrm{Grass}(V, d) = \Quot^{d,\mathcal{O}_S}_{V/S/S}\) hold by
258	  construction, so the structural correspondence is preserved automatically
259	  when the underlying \(\Quot\) construction is later filled in, and the
260	  Grassmannian inherits its functoriality and equivalence relation from the
261	  Quot functor rather than restating them.
262	\end{definition}

339	  Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme, \(V\) a locally free \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-module
340	  of rank \(r\), and \(1 \le d \le r\). The functor \(\mathrm{Grass}(V,d)\) of
341	  \cref{def:grassmannian_scheme} is representable by a smooth projective
342	  \(S\)-scheme \(\mathrm{Gr}_S(V, d) \to S\) of relative dimension \(d(r-d)\),
343	  equipped with a tautological quotient
344	  \(\pi^* V \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{U}\) of rank \(d\). The determinant
345	  line bundle \(\det(\mathcal{U})\) is relatively very ample, and the
346	  associated linear system gives a closed embedding
347	  \(\mathrm{Gr}_S(V, d) \hookrightarrow
348	  \mathbb{P}_S\!\left(\bigwedge^d V\right)\) -- the \emph{Pl\"ucker embedding}.
349	
350	  \emph{Faithful target.} The representability conclusion is exactly Mathlib's
351	  representability predicate (\cref{lem:functor_is_representable_mathlib}): the
352	  intended statement is \((\mathrm{Grass}(V,d)).\mathrm{IsRepresentable}\), whose
353	  unfolding \texttt{Functor.IsRepresentable.has\_representation} is precisely
354	  \(\exists\,Y : \mathrm{Over}\,S,\ \mathrm{Nonempty}\bigl((\mathrm{Grass}(V,d)).\mathrm{RepresentableBy}\,Y\bigr)\).
355	  Stating the theorem in the \texttt{IsRepresentable} spelling lets the parent
356	  project recover the representing \(S\)-scheme and the Yoneda bijection
357	  directly through \texttt{Functor.representableBy} / \texttt{Functor.reprX} at
358	  merge-back, instead of re-bridging an ad-hoc existential.
359	
360	  \emph{Universe constraint.} \(\mathrm{RepresentableBy}\,F\,Y\) is available
361	  only for \(F : \mathcal{C}^{op} \to \mathrm{Type}\,v\) whose representing
362	  object \(Y\) lies in the \emph{same} category \(\mathcal{C}\) at the
363	  \emph{matching} hom-universe \(v\). Hence the target universe of the
364	  Grassmannian functor (and of the Quot functor of \cref{def:quot_functor} on
365	  which it is built) must be pinned to the hom-universe of the schemes category
366	  \(\mathrm{Sch}/S\) -- equivalently of \(\mathrm{Over}\,S\), in which the
367	  representing object \(\mathrm{Gr}_S(V,d)\) lives. If the functor's value
368	  universe is left free, the representability statement does not typecheck.
369	\end{theorem}

372	% [Nitsure], \S 1 (references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex,
373	% L773-L930), which gives the construction in five named steps

378	\begin{proof}
379	  
380	  Over \(S = \Spec \mathbb{Z}\) and \(V = \mathcal{O}_S^{\oplus r}\) the
381	  Grassmannian \(\mathrm{Gr}(r, d)\) is constructed by gluing
382	  \(\binom{r}{d}\) affine charts \(U^I = \Spec\mathbb{Z}[X^I]\) indexed by
383	  size-\(d\) subsets \(I \subseteq \{1, \dots, r\}\). The chart \(U^I\)
384	  carries the matrix \(X^I\) of size \(d \times r\) whose \(I\)-th \(d \times d\)
385	  minor is the identity, with the remaining \(d(r-d)\) entries free
386	  variables (so \(U^I \cong \mathbb{A}^{d(r-d)}_{\mathbb{Z}}\)). The
387	  transition maps \(\theta_{I,J} : U^I \cap U^J \to U^J \cap U^I\) defined
388	  by \(X^J \mapsto (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\) on the locus where \(\det X^I_J\) is
389	  invertible satisfy the cocycle condition
390	  \(\theta_{I,K} = \theta_{I,J} \circ \theta_{J,K}\). The glued
391	  \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec\mathbb{Z}\) is smooth of relative dimension
392	  \(d(r-d)\). Separatedness is checked from the diagonal
393	  \(\Delta_{I,J} \subset U^I \times U^J\) cut out by \(X^J_I X^I - X^J = 0\).
394	  Properness follows from the valuative criterion for DVRs: given
395	  \(\varphi : \Spec K \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) over a DVR \(R\) with fraction
396	  field \(K\), factoring through some chart \(U^I\) via a ring map
397	  \(f : \mathbb{Z}[X^I] \to K\), choose \(J\) minimising the valuation
398	  \(\nu(f(P^I_J))\) of the minor; on the chart \(U^J\) all entries have
399	  non-negative valuation, so \(f\) extends uniquely to \(\Spec R\).
400	
401	  The universal quotient \(u : \mathcal{O}_{\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)}^{\oplus r}
402	  \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{U}\) is given on \(U^I\) by the matrix \(X^I\)
403	  and glued by \(g_{I,J} = (X^I_J)^{-1} \in GL_d(U^I \cap U^J)\). The
404	  determinant line bundle \(\det\mathcal{U}\) has transition functions
405	  \(\det g_{I,J} = 1/P^I_J\); the global sections
406	  \(\sigma_I \in \Gamma(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d), \det\mathcal{U})\) defined by
407	  \(\sigma_I|_{U^J} = P^J_I\) form a base-point-free linear system separating
408	  points over \(\Spec\mathbb{Z}\), giving an embedding into
409	  \(\mathbb{P}^{\binom{r}{d}-1}_{\mathbb{Z}}\), which is closed by
410	  properness. Representability of \(\mathrm{Grass}(r,d)\) is then verified
411	  on the universal pair \((\mathrm{Gr}(r,d), u)\): a quotient
412	  \(q : \mathcal{O}_T^{\oplus r} \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\) of
413	  rank \(d\) on \(T\) is everywhere locally trivialised; on the locus where
414	  the columns indexed by \(I\) are a basis the matrix of \(q\) has the form
415	  \(X^I\), defining the classifying morphism \(T \to U^I \subset
416	  \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\). Base change from \(\mathbb{Z}\) to a general noetherian
417	  base \(S\) with a vector bundle \(V\) is direct: trivialise \(V\) on an open
418	  cover and glue. For non-trivial \(V\) the same construction produces
419	  \(\mathrm{Gr}_S(V, d)\) with Pl\"ucker embedding into
420	  \(\mathbb{P}_S(\bigwedge^d V)\).
421	\end{proof}

423	\section{Lean encoding}
424	\label{sec:quot_lean_encoding}
425	
426	The Lean target file is
427	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/QuotScheme.lean}. The Quot-foundations
428	layer proceeds in two phases.
429	
430	\paragraph{Phase 1: Hilbert polynomial (\cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}).}
431	On a finite-type morphism \(\pi : X \to S\) with \(S\) noetherian and a
432	coherent \(\mathcal{F}\) on \(X\) with proper support over \(S\), the per-fiber
433	Hilbert polynomial is encoded as a function \(s \mapsto
434	\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s} \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\). Mathlib provides
435	Euler-characteristic infrastructure on coherent sheaves on noetherian
436	schemes; the polynomial property of
437	\(m \mapsto \chi(\mathcal{F} \otimes L^{\otimes m})\) requires
438	Snapper's Lemma, which is a project-side sub-build on top of the
439	graded-Euler-characteristic infrastructure.
440	
441	\emph{Intended signature of \(\mathrm{hilbertPolynomial}\).} Mathlib at the pin
442	carries no single \texttt{IsCoherent} predicate; coherence of \(\mathcal{F}\)
443	over the locally noetherian base is the conjunction of the two predicates that
444	do exist, \texttt{SheafOfModules.IsQuasicoherent} and
445	\texttt{SheafOfModules.IsFiniteType} -- i.e.\ the hypotheses
446	\([\mathcal{F}.\mathrm{IsQuasicoherent}]\) and
447	\([\mathcal{F}.\mathrm{IsFiniteType}]\) (and likewise for the line bundle
448	\(L\)). The properness-of-support hypothesis
449	``\(\operatorname{supp}\mathcal{F}\) proper over \(S\)'' has \emph{no} clean
450	Mathlib predicate. The minimal honest encoding forms the schematic support of
451	\(\mathcal{F}\) as a closed subscheme \(Z \hookrightarrow X\) and requires the
452	composite \(Z \hookrightarrow X \xrightarrow{\pi} S\) to satisfy
453	\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.IsProper}; since the schematic-support closed
454	subscheme of a coherent sheaf is not itself available at the pin, this support
455	datum is recorded as a project-side gap to be supplied alongside the
456	definition. (The existing Lean pin
457	\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.hilbertPolynomial} is retained; adding these
458	hypotheses to its signature is a later refactor of the \texttt{.lean} stub, not
459	done in this pass.)
460	
461	\paragraph{Phase 2: Quot and Grassmannian functors
462	(\cref{def:quot_functor}, \cref{def:grassmannian_scheme},
463	\cref{thm:grassmannian_representable}).} The Quot functor
464	\(\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\) is encoded as a contravariant functor on
465	\((\mathrm{Sch}/S)\) of \(T\)-flat coherent quotients; the Grassmannian
466	\(\mathrm{Grass}(V, d)\) is the special case \(X = S\), \(E = V\), constant
467	Hilbert polynomial \(\Phi = d\). The representing scheme
468	\(\mathrm{Gr}_S(V, d)\) is the relative gluing of \(\binom{r}{d}\)
469	affine charts \(U^I\) (each isomorphic to a relative affine space
470	\(\mathbb{A}^{d(r-d)}_S\)) via the Pl\"ucker cocycle \(\theta_{I,J}\). The
471	gluing backbone is \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.GlueData}. The
472	universal quotient sheaf \(\mathcal{U}\) is glued by the transition cocycle
473	\(g_{I,J} = (X^I_J)^{-1}\), and the determinant line bundle
474	\(\det \mathcal{U}\) gives the Pl\"ucker closed embedding into
475	\(\mathbb{P}_S(\bigwedge^d V)\). The representability statement is pinned to
476	Mathlib's \texttt{CategoryTheory.Functor.IsRepresentable}
477	(\cref{lem:functor_is_representable_mathlib}), which packages exactly the
478	\texttt{RepresentableBy} witness on the universal pair
479	\((\mathrm{Gr}_S(V, d), \mathcal{U})\), in the same Yoneda style as
480	\cref{chap:Picard_RelativeSpec}.
481	
482	\emph{Intended signatures.} Coherence of \(E\) in \(\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\) is
483	encoded as in Phase 1, by the conjunction \([E.\mathrm{IsQuasicoherent}]\) and
484	\([E.\mathrm{IsFiniteType}]\) over the locally noetherian base (no single
485	\texttt{IsCoherent} predicate exists at the pin). For \(\mathrm{Grass}(V, d)\),
486	the hypothesis ``\(V\) locally free of rank \(r\)'' has no faithful Mathlib
487	predicate at the pin: \texttt{SheafOfModules.IsLocallyFree} appears only
488	upstream of the pinned commit and is in any case rank-agnostic (it fixes no
489	value \(r\)). The intended encoding therefore pairs an
490	\texttt{IsLocallyFree}-style hypothesis on \(V\) with an explicit rank
491	\(r : \mathbb{N}\) and the constraint \(1 \le d \le r\) carried as a hypothesis
492	on naturals; the rank-\(r\) local-freeness predicate is flagged as a
493	project-side gap to be supplied. Consistently with
494	\cref{def:grassmannian_scheme}, \(\mathrm{Grass}(V, d)\) is \emph{defined} as
495	\(\Quot^{d,\mathcal{O}_S}_{V/S/S}\) (the case \(X = S\), \(E = V\),
496	\(\Phi = d\)) rather than as an independent functor. The existing Lean pins on
497	\(\Quot\) and \(\mathrm{Grass}\) are retained; threading these hypotheses
498	through the \texttt{.lean} signatures is a later refactor.
499	
500	\section{Out of scope}
501	\label{sec:quot_out_of_scope}
502	
503	This chapter packages \emph{only} the Quot-foundations layer: the Hilbert
504	polynomial, the Quot functor, and the Grassmannian scheme with its
505	representability. The following are explicitly out of scope here:
506	\begin{itemize}
507	  \item \textbf{Representability of the Quot scheme} -- the
508	    Grothendieck--Altman--Kleiman existence theorem and its proof skeleton

513	  \item \textbf{Hilbert scheme as a separate chapter}. The Hilbert scheme

517	  \item \textbf{Snapper's Lemma} -- the polynomial-eventually property of

522	    Grassmannian variants} (Nitsure \S 1, Exercises (1)--(4)) --

524	  \item \textbf{Group-scheme structure on \(\Pic_{C/k}\)} (A.2.c) -- the

531	  \item \textbf{Identity-component \(\Pic^0\) and abelian-variety
532	    structure} (A.3) -- a separate Route A sub-row, fed by but not

448	\centerline{\large\bf Stratification by Hilbert Polynomials}
449	
450	\medskip
451	
452	
453	Let $X$ be a finite type scheme over
454	a field $k$, together with a line bundle $L$.
455	Recall that if $F$ is a coherent sheaf on 
456	$X$ whose support is proper over $k$, then
457	the \textbf{Hilbert polynomial} ${\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]$ of $F$ is defined by
458	the function 
459	$${\Phi}(m) = \chi(F(m)) = 
460	\sum_{i=0}^n (-1)^i \dim_k H^i(X, F\otimes L^{\otimes m})$$
461	where the dimensions of the cohomologies are finite because of the 
462	coherence and properness conditions. The fact that $\chi(F(m))$
463	is indeed a polynomial in $m$ under the above assumption is a special
464	case of what is known as Snapper's Lemma (see Kleiman [K] for a proof).
465	
466	
467	
468	Let $X\to S$ be a finite type morphism of noetherian schemes,
469	and let $L$ be a line bundle on $X$. Let $\F$
470	be any coherent sheaf on 
471	$X$ whose schematic support is proper over $S$. Then for each $s\in S$,
472	we get a polynomial ${\Phi}_s \in  \Q[\lambda]$ 
473	which is the Hilbert polynomial of
474	the restriction $\F_s = F|_{X_s}$ of $\F$ to the fiber $X_s$ over $s$,
475	calculated with respect to the line bundle $L_s = L|_{X_s}$. 
476	If $\F$ is flat over $S$ then the function $s\mapsto {\Phi}_s$ 
477	from the set of points of $S$ to the polynomial ring $\Q[\lambda]$ is 
478	known to be locally constant on $S$. 
479	
480	
481	This shows that the functor $\Quot_{E/X/S}$ naturally decomposes
482	as a co-product 
483	$$\Quot_{E/X/S} = \coprod_{{\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]}\, \Quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}$$
484	where for any polynomial ${\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]$, the functor 
485	$\Quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}$ associates
486	to any $T$ the set of all equivalence classes of 
487	families $\langle \F,q \rangle$ such that at each $t\in T$ the 
488	Hilbert polynomial of the restriction $\F_t$, calculated
489	using the pull-back of $L$, is ${\Phi}$. Correspondingly,
490	the representing scheme  $\quot_{E/X/S}$, when it exists, naturally decomposes
491	as a co-product
492	$$\quot_{E/X/S} = \coprod_{{\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]}\, \quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}$$
493	
494	\textbf{Note } 
495	We will generally take $X$ to be (quasi-)projective over $S$,
496	and $L$ to be a relatively very ample line bundle. Then indeed 
497	the Hilbert and Quot functors are representable by schemes,
498	but not in general.

---existsUnique stmt---

/--
The key property of Hilbert polynomials. If `F` is a field with characteristic `0`, `p : F[X]` and
`d : ℕ`, then for any large enough `n : ℕ`, `(Polynomial.hilbertPoly p d).eval (n : F)` equals the
coefficient of `Xⁿ` in the power series expansion of `p/(1 - X)ᵈ`.
-/
theorem coeff_mul_invOneSubPow_eq_hilbertPoly_eval
    {p : F[X]} (d : ℕ) {n : ℕ} (hn : p.natDegree < n) :
    (p * invOneSubPow F d : F⟦X⟧).coeff n = (hilbertPoly p d).eval (n : F) := by
  delta hilbertPoly; induction d with
  | zero => simp only [invOneSubPow_zero, Units.val_one, mul_one, coeff_coe, eval_zero]
            exact coeff_eq_zero_of_natDegree_lt hn
  | succ d hd =>
      simp only [eval_finset_sum, eval_smul, smul_eq_mul]
      rw [← Finset.sum_coe_sort]
      have h_le (i : p.support) : (i : ℕ) ≤ n :=
        le_trans (le_natDegree_of_ne_zero <| mem_support_iff.1 i.2) hn.le
      have h (i : p.support) : eval ↑n (preHilbertPoly F d ↑i) = (n + d - ↑i).choose d := by
        rw [preHilbertPoly_eq_choose_sub_add _ _ (h_le i), Nat.sub_add_comm (h_le i)]
      simp_rw [h]
      rw [Finset.sum_coe_sort _ (fun x => (p.coeff ↑x) * (_ + d - ↑x).choose _),
        PowerSeries.coeff_mul, Finset.Nat.sum_antidiagonal_eq_sum_range_succ_mk,
        invOneSubPow_val_eq_mk_sub_one_add_choose_of_pos _ _ (zero_lt_succ d)]
      simp only [coeff_coe, coeff_mk]
      symm
      refine Finset.sum_subset_zero_on_sdiff (fun s hs ↦ ?_) (fun x hx ↦ ?_) (fun x hx ↦ ?_)
      · rw [Finset.mem_range_succ_iff]
        exact h_le ⟨s, hs⟩
      · simp only [Finset.mem_sdiff, mem_support_iff, not_not] at hx
        rw [hx.2, zero_mul]
      · rw [add_comm, Nat.add_sub_assoc (h_le ⟨x, hx⟩), succ_eq_add_one, add_tsub_cancel_right]

/--
The polynomial satisfying the key property of `Polynomial.hilbertPoly p d` is unique.
-/
theorem existsUnique_hilbertPoly (p : F[X]) (d : ℕ) :
    ∃! h : F[X], ∃ N : ℕ, ∀ n > N,
      (p * invOneSubPow F d : F⟦X⟧).coeff n = h.eval (n : F) := by
  use hilbertPoly p d; constructor
  · use p.natDegree
    exact fun n => coeff_mul_invOneSubPow_eq_hilbertPoly_eval d
  · rintro h ⟨N, hhN⟩
    apply eq_of_infinite_eval_eq h (hilbertPoly p d)
    apply ((Set.Ioi_infinite (max N p.natDegree)).image cast_injective.injOn).mono
    rintro x ⟨n, hn, rfl⟩
    simp only [Set.mem_Ioi, sup_lt_iff, Set.mem_setOf_eq] at hn ⊢
    rw [← coeff_mul_invOneSubPow_eq_hilbertPoly_eval d hn.2, hhN n hn.1]

input.old_string:
Let \(X \to S\) be a finite-type morphism of noetherian schemes and let \(L\)
  be a line bundle on \(X\). For a coherent sheaf \(\mathcal{F}\) on \(X\) whose
  schematic support is proper over \(S\) and a point \(s \in S\), the
  \emph{Hilbert polynomial of \(\mathcal{F}\) at \(s\) relative to \(L\)} is the
  polynomial \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s} \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\) characterised by
  \[
    \Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}(m)
    \;=\; \chi(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}|_{X_s} \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})
    \;=\; \sum_{i \ge 0} (-1)^i \dim_{\kappa(s)} H^i(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}|_{X_s} \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})
  \]
  (a polynomial in \(m\) by Snapper's Lemma; the sum is finite by coherence and
  properness). When \(\mathcal{F}\) is \(S\)-flat the function
  \(s \mapsto \Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}\) is locally constant on \(S\), so \(\Phi\) is
  well-defined on connected components.
input.new_string:
Let \(X \to S\) be a finite-type morphism of noetherian schemes and let \(L\)
  be a line bundle on \(X\). For a coherent sheaf \(\mathcal{F}\) on \(X\) whose
  schematic support is proper over \(S\) and a point \(s \in S\), write \(X_s\)
  for the fibre over \(s\), \(\mathcal{F}_s = \mathcal{F}|_{X_s}\) and
  \(L_s = L|_{X_s}\). The \emph{Hilbert polynomial of \(\mathcal{F}\) at \(s\)
  relative to \(L\)} is the unique polynomial
  \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s} \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\) that agrees, for all
  \(m \gg 0\), with the graded Hilbert function
  \[
    m \;\longmapsto\; \dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma\bigl(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}\bigr)
  \]
  of the section module
  \(\bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\)
  (\cref{def:sectionGradedModule}), viewed as a finitely generated graded module
  over the homogeneous section ring
  \(\bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\)
  (\cref{def:sectionGradedRing}). Existence and uniqueness of
  \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}\) are the content of
  \cref{thm:hilbertPoly_of_sectionModule}.

  This is the classical Hilbert polynomial. When \(\mathcal{F}_s\) is coherent
  and \(L_s\) is ample on the proper \(\kappa(s)\)-scheme \(X_s\), Serre vanishing
  gives \(H^i(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}) = 0\) for all \(i > 0\)
  and \(m \gg 0\), so for \(m \gg 0\)
  \[
    \dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})
    \;=\; \chi(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})
    \;=\; \sum_{i \ge 0} (-1)^i \dim_{\kappa(s)} H^i(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}),
  \]
  and hence \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}\) coincides with the Euler-characteristic
  Hilbert polynomial of Snapper's Lemma -- no numerical invariant is lost. The
  construction above, however, uses only the global sections \(H^0\): the higher
  cohomology enters solely through the classical statement that the two functions
  agree for \(m \gg 0\), never through the definition of \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}\)
  itself. When \(\mathcal{F}\) is \(S\)-flat the function
  \(s \mapsto \Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}\) is locally constant on \(S\), so \(\Phi\) is
  well-defined on connected components.

\end{definition}

\section{Graded Hilbert polynomial}
\label{sec:graded_hilbert_polynomial}

This section gives the graded-Hilbert-function construction that underlies
\cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}. Working over a single fibre \(X_s\) with its
ample line bundle \(L_s\) and coherent sheaf \(\mathcal{F}_s\), it builds the
section graded ring (\cref{def:sectionGradedRing}) and module
(\cref{def:sectionGradedModule}), records their finite generation
(\cref{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg}), and extracts the Hilbert polynomial
(\cref{thm:hilbertPoly_of_sectionModule}) by combining the graded
Hilbert--Serre rationality of the Hilbert series
(\cref{lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational}) with Mathlib's polynomial-extraction
lemma (\cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}). The route uses only \(H^0\)
(global sections), so it is independent of higher coherent cohomology.

\begin{definition}
[Section graded ring]
  \label{def:sectionGradedRing}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.sectionGradedRing}
  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
  % sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert Polynomials" (read from
  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L453-L464).
  % SOURCE QUOTE:
  % "Let $X$ be a finite type scheme over
  %  a field $k$, together with a line bundle $L$.
  %  Recall that if $F$ is a coherent sheaf on
  %  $X$ whose support is proper over $k$, then
  %  the \textbf{Hilbert polynomial} ${\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]$ of $F$ is defined by
  %  the function
  %  $${\Phi}(m) = \chi(F(m)) =
  %  \sum_{i=0}^n (-1)^i \dim_k H^i(X, F\otimes L^{\otimes m})$$
  %  where the dimensions of the cohomologies are finite because of the
  %  coherence and properness conditions."
  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5); the section graded
  ring is the homogeneous coordinate ring of [Hartshorne], I.7.}
  Let \(s \in S\) and let \(L_s\) be a line bundle on the fibre \(X_s\), a scheme
  of finite type over the residue field \(\kappa(s)\). The \emph{section graded
  ring} of \(L_s\) is the graded commutative ring
  \[
    R(X_s, L_s) \;=\; \bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma\bigl(X_s,\, L_s^{\otimes m}\bigr),
  \]
  with degree-\(m\) component \(\Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\) and multiplication
  the tensor product of sections
  \(\Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m}) \otimes_{\kappa(s)}
  \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m'}) \to \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes (m+m')})\),
  \(\sigma \otimes \tau \mapsto \sigma \cdot \tau\). Its degree-zero component is
  \(\Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{O}_{X_s})\), which contains \(\kappa(s)\). When \(X_s\) is
  proper over \(\kappa(s)\) and \(L_s\) is ample, \(R(X_s, L_s)\) is a finitely
  generated -- hence Noetherian -- graded \(\kappa(s)\)-algebra (a suitable
  Veronese power of \(L_s\) is very ample and embeds \(X_s\) into a projective
  space, exhibiting \(R(X_s, L_s)\) up to a finite extension as a quotient of a
  polynomial ring).
\end{definition}

\begin{definition}
[Section graded module]
  \label{def:sectionGradedModule}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.sectionGradedModule}
  \uses{def:sectionGradedRing}
  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
  % Polynomials" (read from
  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L453-L464).
  % SOURCE QUOTE:
  % "Recall that if $F$ is a coherent sheaf on
  %  $X$ whose support is proper over $k$, then
  %  the \textbf{Hilbert polynomial} ${\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]$ of $F$ is defined by
  %  the function
  %  $${\Phi}(m) = \chi(F(m)) =
  %  \sum_{i=0}^n (-1)^i \dim_k H^i(X, F\otimes L^{\otimes m})$$
  %  where the dimensions of the cohomologies are finite because of the
  %  coherence and properness conditions."
  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5); cf.\ [Hartshorne],
  I.7.}
  Let \(\mathcal{F}_s\) be a coherent sheaf on the fibre \(X_s\) and \(L_s\) a
  line bundle on \(X_s\). The \emph{section graded module} of \(\mathcal{F}_s\)
  twisted by \(L_s\) is the graded \(R(X_s, L_s)\)-module
  (\cref{def:sectionGradedRing})
  \[
    M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)
    \;=\; \bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma\bigl(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}\bigr),
  \]
  with degree-\(m\) component the \(\kappa(s)\)-vector space
  \(\Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\) and scalar action given
  by the section tensor product
  \(\Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m}) \otimes_{\kappa(s)}
  \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m'})
  \to \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes (m+m')})\). Its graded Hilbert
  function is \(m \mapsto \dim_{\kappa(s)}
  \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\).
\end{definition}

\begin{lemma}
[Finite generation of the section graded module]
  \label{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.sectionGradedModule_fg}
  \uses{def:sectionGradedModule, def:sectionGradedRing}
  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
  % Polynomials" (read from
  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L453-L464).
  % SOURCE QUOTE:
  % "Recall that if $F$ is a coherent sheaf on
  %  $X$ whose support is proper over $k$, then
  %  the \textbf{Hilbert polynomial} ${\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]$ of $F$ is defined by
  %  the function
  %  $${\Phi}(m) = \chi(F(m)) =
  %  \sum_{i=0}^n (-1)^i \dim_k H^i(X, F\otimes L^{\otimes m})$$
  %  where the dimensions of the cohomologies are finite because of the
  %  coherence and properness conditions."
  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5); Serre's theorem on
  ample line bundles, [Hartshorne], I.7 / II.5.}
  Let \(X_s\) be proper over the field \(\kappa(s)\), let \(L_s\) be an ample line
  bundle on \(X_s\), and let \(\mathcal{F}_s\) be a coherent sheaf on \(X_s\).
  Then the section graded ring \(R(X_s, L_s)\) (\cref{def:sectionGradedRing}) is a
  Noetherian graded ring, and the section graded module
  \(M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)\) (\cref{def:sectionGradedModule}) is a finitely
  generated graded \(R(X_s, L_s)\)-module. In particular each component
  \(\Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\) is a finite-dimensional
  \(\kappa(s)\)-vector space.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{def:sectionGradedRing, def:sectionGradedModule}
  Since \(L_s\) is ample and \(X_s\) is proper over \(\kappa(s)\), a Veronese
  power \(L_s^{\otimes e}\) is very ample and gives a closed immersion
  \(X_s \hookrightarrow \mathbb{P}^N_{\kappa(s)}\). By Serre's theorem the section
  graded ring \(R(X_s, L_s)\) is a finitely generated graded
  \(\kappa(s)\)-algebra, so it is Noetherian. For the coherent sheaf
  \(\mathcal{F}_s\), the same ampleness yields, again by Serre, that
  \(\bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\) is a
  finitely generated graded module over \(R(X_s, L_s)\): the twists
  \(\mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}\) are globally generated for
  \(m \gg 0\), and the finitely many generating sections in low degrees together
  with the multiplication maps generate \(M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)\) over
  \(R(X_s, L_s)\). Finite dimensionality of each component over \(\kappa(s)\)
  follows from coherence and properness (finiteness of cohomology of coherent
  sheaves on a proper scheme over a field).
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}[Existence and uniqueness of the Hilbert polynomial of a rational
  graded series]
  \label{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}
  \lean{Polynomial.existsUnique_hilbertPoly}
  \mathlibok
  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
  (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Polynomial.HilbertPoly}).}
  Let \(F\) be a field of characteristic zero, let \(p \in F[X]\), and let
  \(d \in \mathbb{N}\). Then there is a unique polynomial \(h \in F[X]\) such that
  for some \(N \in \mathbb{N}\) and all \(n > N\) the coefficient of \(X^n\) in the
  power-series expansion of \(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d} \in F\llbracket X\rrbracket\)
  equals \(h(n)\):
  \[
    \exists!\, h \in F[X],\ \exists N,\ \forall n > N : \;
    \bigl[ X^n \bigr]\bigl( p \cdot (1-X)^{-d} \bigr) \;=\; h(n).
  \]
  Equivalently, the eventual coefficient function of any rational power series
  with denominator a power of \(1 - X\) is, for \(n \gg 0\), given by a unique
  polynomial \(h\) (Mathlib's \(\mathrm{hilbertPoly}\,p\,d\)). The hypothesis
  \([\mathrm{CharZero}\,F]\) is satisfied by \(F = \mathbb{Q}\), which is the
  instance used to obtain \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s} \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\).
\end{lemma}

\begin{lemma}[Graded Hilbert--Serre: rationality of the Hilbert series]
  \label{lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gradedModule_hilbertSeries_rational}
  \uses{def:sectionGradedRing, def:sectionGradedModule, lem:sectionGradedModule_fg}
  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
  % Polynomials" (read from
  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L459-L464).
  % SOURCE QUOTE:
  % "$${\Phi}(m) = \chi(F(m)) =
  %  \sum_{i=0}^n (-1)^i \dim_k H^i(X, F\otimes L^{\otimes m})$$
  %  where the dimensions of the cohomologies are finite because of the
  %  coherence and properness conditions. The fact that $\chi(F(m))$
  %  is indeed a polynomial in $m$ under the above assumption is a special
  %  case of what is known as Snapper's Lemma (see Kleiman [K] for a proof)."
  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5); Hilbert--Serre,
  [Hartshorne], I.7.}
  Let \(R\) be a Noetherian graded ring whose degree-zero part is a field
  \(\kappa\) and which is generated as a \(\kappa\)-algebra by finitely many
  elements, and let \(M\) be a finitely generated graded \(R\)-module with
  finite-dimensional graded components \(M_n\). Then there exist
  \(p \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) and \(d \in \mathbb{N}\) and an \(N \in \mathbb{N}\)
  such that for all \(n > N\)
  \[
    \dim_{\kappa} M_n \;=\; \bigl[ X^n \bigr]\bigl( p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d}\bigr);
  \]
  that is, the Hilbert series \(\sum_{n \ge 0} (\dim_\kappa M_n)\, X^n\) is, up to
  a polynomial correction in low degrees, the rational function
  \(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d}\). Applied to \(R = R(X_s, L_s)\) and
  \(M = M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)\) (finitely generated by
  \cref{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg}), this exhibits the graded Hilbert function
  \(m \mapsto \dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\)
  as the eventual coefficient function of such a rational series.

  \emph{Status.} This is the substantive half of the graded Hilbert--Serre
  theorem (rationality of the Hilbert series of a finitely generated graded
  module). It is a project-side obligation: Mathlib's
  \cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib} supplies only the converse
  polynomial-extraction step from a rational series, not the production of the
  rational series from the module.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg}
  By \cref{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg} the ring \(R = R(X_s, L_s)\) is Noetherian
  and finitely generated over \(\kappa = \kappa(s)\), say by homogeneous
  generators \(x_1, \dots, x_r\) of positive degrees, and
  \(M = M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)\) is a finitely generated graded \(R\)-module
  with finite-dimensional components. Passing to a Veronese subalgebra and
  re-grading reduces to the case where \(R\) is generated in degree \(1\), so
  \(R\) is a graded quotient of a polynomial ring
  \(\kappa[x_1, \dots, x_r]\). One proves rationality of the Hilbert series by
  induction on \(r\): multiplication by \(x_r\) gives a short exact sequence of
  graded modules
  \(0 \to K \to M(-1) \xrightarrow{x_r} M \to C \to 0\)
  with \(K, C\) finitely generated graded modules annihilated by \(x_r\), hence
  modules over a polynomial ring in \(r - 1\) variables; additivity of
  \(\dim_\kappa\) on the graded components turns the inductive hypothesis for
  \(K, C\) into the statement that
  \((1 - X) \cdot \sum_n (\dim_\kappa M_n) X^n\) is, up to a polynomial, of the
  form \(q \cdot (1 - X)^{-(d-1)}\), whence
  \(\sum_n (\dim_\kappa M_n) X^n = p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d}\) up to a polynomial. The
  base case \(r = 0\) has \(M\) finite-dimensional, so \(\dim_\kappa M_n = 0\) for
  \(n \gg 0\) and the series is a polynomial. Choosing \(p \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\)
  (the integer coefficients viewed in \(\mathbb{Q}\)) gives the claim.
\end{proof}

\begin{theorem}[Hilbert polynomial from the section module]
  \label{thm:hilbertPoly_of_sectionModule}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.hilbertPolynomialOfSectionModule}
  \uses{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg, lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational,
    lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}
  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
  % Polynomials" (read from
  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L468-L478).
  % SOURCE QUOTE:
  % "Let $X\to S$ be a finite type morphism of noetherian schemes,
  %  and let $L$ be a line bundle on $X$. Let $\F$
  %  be any coherent sheaf on
  %  $X$ whose schematic support is proper over $S$. Then for each $s\in S$,
  %  we get a polynomial ${\Phi}_s \in  \Q[\lambda]$
  %  which is the Hilbert polynomial of
  %  the restriction $\F_s = F|_{X_s}$ of $\F$ to the fiber $X_s$ over $s$,
  %  calculated with respect to the line bundle $L_s = L|_{X_s}$."
  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
  Let \(X_s\) be proper over the field \(\kappa(s)\), let \(L_s\) be an ample line
  bundle on \(X_s\), and let \(\mathcal{F}_s\) be a coherent sheaf on \(X_s\).
  Then there is a unique polynomial \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s} \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\)
  such that for all \(m \gg 0\)
  \[
    \Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}(m)
    \;=\; \dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma\bigl(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}\bigr).
  \]
  This \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}\) is the Hilbert polynomial of
  \cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}.
\end{theorem}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg, lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational,
    lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}
  By \cref{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg} the section graded module
  \(M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)\) is a finitely generated graded module over the
  Noetherian graded ring \(R(X_s, L_s)\), with finite-dimensional components. By
  \cref{lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational} there are \(p \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) and
  \(d \in \mathbb{N}\) and an \(N\) with
  \(\dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})
  = [X^m]\,(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d})\) for \(m > N\). Apply
  \cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib} over the characteristic-zero field
  \(F = \mathbb{Q}\) to this pair \((p, d)\): it yields a unique polynomial
  \(h \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) whose values \(h(n)\) eventually equal
  \([X^n]\,(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d})\). Setting
  \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s} := h\) (with the variable renamed \(\lambda\)) gives a
  polynomial with \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}(m) = \dim_{\kappa(s)}
  \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\) for \(m \gg 0\). Uniqueness
  is the \(\exists!\) clause of \cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}: any two
  polynomials agreeing with the Hilbert function for \(m \gg 0\) agree at
  infinitely many points, hence are equal.
\end{proof}

\label{sec:quot_functor}

## Strategy-modifying findings
**The graded encoding's pivot lemma is only HALF in Mathlib — the substantive half
(Hilbert–Serre rationality) is a project-side gap, not a Mathlib export.**

The directive describes `Polynomial.existsUnique_hilbertPoly` as
"(graded Hilbert–Serre)" and frames the strategy as routing polynomiality
"directly through Mathlib's already-verified" lemma. I read the actual Mathlib
source (`Mathlib/RingTheory/Polynomial/HilbertPoly.lean`). The lemma is:

```
theorem existsUnique_hilbertPoly (p : F[X]) (d : ℕ) [Field F] [CharZero F] :
  ∃! h : F[X], ∃ N, ∀ n > N, (p * invOneSubPow F d : F⟦X⟧).coeff n = h.eval (n : F)
```

This is **purely the polynomial-extraction step**: given a numerator `p` and a
denominator `(1-X)^d`, the eventual coefficients of `p·(1-X)^{-d}` are a unique
polynomial. It says **nothing** about graded modules. The genuine Hilbert–Serre
content — that the Hilbert function `n ↦ dim_κ M_n` of a *finitely generated
graded module M over a Noetherian graded ring* equals the coefficients of some
rational series `p·(1-X)^{-d}` — is **absent from Mathlib** (grep for
`hilbert.?serre`/`hilbert series`/`hilbertSeries` over the whole pinned Mathlib:
no hits; the `HilbertPoly` file contains only `preHilbertPoly`, `hilbertPoly`,
`coeff_mul_invOneSubPow_eq_hilbertPoly_eval`, `existsUnique_hilbertPoly`,
`eq_hilbertPoly_of_forall_coeff_eq_eval`, and degree lemmas).

**Consequence for the route.** `thm:hilbertPoly_of_sectionModule` needs a bridge
`dim_κ M_n  ⟶  p·(1-X)^{-d}` *before* it can invoke `existsUnique_hilbertPoly`.
I encoded that bridge as the new project-own lemma
`lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational` (`\lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gradedModule_hilbertSeries_rational}`,
NOT `\mathlibok`). It is a real proof obligation (classical Hilbert–Serre,
inductive on the number of degree-1 generators / dévissage). This is mathematically
routine and still **Čech-independent / H^0-only** (so the strategy's core claim —
avoiding higher cohomology — holds), but it is **not free from Mathlib** and
should be costed as project work, not a Mathlib citation.

Recommended STRATEGY.md adjustment: under "Routes QUOT", record that the graded
encoding depends on TWO pieces — (a) Mathlib `existsUnique_hilbertPoly`
(extraction, done) and (b) an in-project graded Hilbert–Serre rationality lemma
(`gradedModule_hilbertSeries_rational`, to be proved) — rather than a single
Mathlib lemma. Also check whether Mathlib's graded-module / `Module.length` /
associated-graded machinery shortens (b); a dedicated `reference-retriever` or
Lean search pass on `Mathlib`'s graded-module length-additivity lemmas would
de-risk it before any QUOT prover dispatch.
